coredata.dc:title: Vision-powered generative paradigm for robust battery health prediction in real-world electric vehicles

Li, Minghe
Zhang, Zijun
Tsui, Kwok-Leung
coredata.dc:description: Predicting the state of health (SOH) of electric vehicle (EV) batteries under real-world operating conditions remains challenging due to noisy measurements, irregular charging behaviors, and highly variable environments, which differ substantially from controlled laboratory settings. This study proposes a vision-powered, multi-modal generative paradigm for accurate EV battery SOH prediction, which reformulates numerical SOH signals into image-based visual representations and leverages pixel-level spatial correlations learned from clean data to reconstruct underlying degradation patterns from noisy EV measurements. The paradigm comprises four key stages: image synthesis, generative model construction, pixel mapping, and SOH prediction. The proposed generative framework integrates a U-Net-based visual channel with a numerical temporal channel, allowing local spatial degradation patterns to be effectively fused with multivariate temporal dynamics. Advanced attention mechanisms are further incorporated to selectively enhance degradation-related representations and to facilitate coherent cross-modal information fusion. The proposed method is evaluated on one laboratory dataset and three real-world EV datasets encompassing diverse battery chemistries, vehicle types, and operational conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed paradigm effectively mitigates data quality limitations inherent in field measurements and achieves highly accurate SOH prediction, attaining a minimum root mean-square error of 0.0013. Comprehensive benchmarking against 15 state-of-the-art methods further confirms its consistently superior generalization performance across heterogeneous datasets. Interpretability analyses further reveal that both visual and numerical modalities capture physics-consistent aging patterns. These findings highlight the potential of the proposed vision-powered paradigm to extract stable degradation signatures from noisy EV operational data and its applicability to broader health prognostics tasks.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Noisy SOH trajectories and EMD-derived trend and noise representations for five EVs in dataset II

The left panels of (A)–(E) present the SOH values computed using the Ampere-hour integration formulation in Equation 1 (magenta markers), together with the smooth degradation trends extracted using EMD (blue curves). The right panels of (A)–(E) show the corresponding noise distributions, defined as residuals between the calculated SOH values and the EMD-derived trends, along with fitted Gaussian curves characterized by near-zero means and small standard deviations.
Overall framework of the proposed vision-powered generative paradigm for EV battery SOH prediction

The workflow consists of four stages: (A) noise analysis of SOH measurements from real-world EV operating conditions; (B) image synthesis based on laboratory degradation data through patch extraction, noise injection, and axis calibration; (C) vision-powered model learning via dual-channel encoding, attention-based feature fusion, and pixel-to-SOH coordinate mapping; and (D) deployment for real-world EV battery health prognostics supported by transfer learning across heterogeneous EV fleets.
Detailed architecture of the CPDG model
SOH prediction results on laboratory cells and real-world EV datasets

(A) Prediction errors (RMSE and MAE) for eight laboratory test cells; error bars represent the standard deviation across 10 repeated runs. (B) SOH prediction trajectories for three representative laboratory test cells, with inset plots showing the distributions of RMSE and MAE. (C) Prediction errors (RMSE and MAE) for vehicles from three real-world EV battery datasets; error bars represent the standard deviation across 10 repeated runs. (D) SOH prediction trajectories for one representative vehicle from each real-world EV dataset, with inset plots showing the distributions of RMSE and MAE.
Comparison of SOH prediction results on three real-world EV datasets

(A–C) Comparison of SOH prediction trajectories for three representative vehicles selected from datasets II, III, and IV, respectively. Each panel shows the EMD-processed SOH curve (Reference), the proposed vision-powered generative paradigm (Proposed), and seven traditional numerical fitting baselines.

(D–F) The corresponding absolute prediction error distributions for the proposed method and eight data-driven benchmark models using violin plots. For each EV dataset, the violin plots depict the empirical error distributions together with the embedded box-and-whisker summaries.
Visualization of the internal feature learning process within the visual channel of the proposed framework
(A–D) The noisy SOH input image and encoder feature maps.
(E) The fused representation after the CBAM attention module.
(F–H) Decoder feature maps illustrating the progressive refinement of the degradation trajectory.

(I–L) (I) The model prediction, (J) the smoothed ground truth, (K) the pixel-wise error map, and (L) overlays the spatial attention map on the input image to highlight regions prioritized by the model.
Analysis of stage-resolved visual representations and SHAP-based numerical feature interpretability

(A–C) Visual feature maps learned by the visual channel for a representative EV sample at the early, middle, and late aging stages, respectively, illustrating the stage-resolved evolution of the learned degradation representations.
(D) SHAP summary plot illustrating the distribution and impact of the top numerical features on the model output, where color indicates the feature value from low to high.
(E) Normalized SHAP importance scores of the top numerical features across different temporal aging phases (early, middle, and late), highlighting stage-dependent variations in feature contributions.
Comparison of prediction accuracy across methods on three different EV datasets

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Predicting the state of health (SOH) of electric vehicle (EV) batteries under real-world operating conditions remains challenging due to noisy measurements, irregular charging behaviors, and highly variable environments, which differ substantially from controlled laboratory settings. This study proposes a vision-powered, multi-modal generative paradigm for accurate EV battery SOH prediction, which reformulates numerical SOH signals into image-based visual representations and leverages pixel-level spatial correlations learned from clean data to reconstruct underlying degradation patterns from noisy EV measurements. The paradigm comprises four key stages: image synthesis, generative model construction, pixel mapping, and SOH prediction. The proposed generative framework integrates a U-Net-based visual channel with a numerical temporal channel, allowing local spatial degradation patterns to be effectively fused with multivariate temporal dynamics. Advanced attention mechanisms are further incorporated to selectively enhance degradation-related representations and to facilitate coherent cross-modal information fusion. The proposed method is evaluated on one laboratory dataset and three real-world EV datasets encompassing diverse battery chemistries, vehicle types, and operational conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed paradigm effectively mitigates data quality limitations inherent in field measurements and achieves highly accurate SOH prediction, attaining a minimum root mean-square error of 0.0013. Comprehensive benchmarking against 15 state-of-the-art methods further confirms its consistently superior generalization performance across heterogeneous datasets. Interpretability analyses further reveal that both visual and numerical modalities capture physics-consistent aging patterns. These findings highlight the potential of the proposed vision-powered paradigm to extract stable degradation signatures from noisy EV operational data and its applicability to broader health prognostics tasks.

ce:list-item.ce:para: A vision-powered generative paradigm predicts battery state of health (SOH) in real-world electric vehicles.
Battery signals are transformed into visual representations to identify degradation patterns amid noise.
Degradation knowledge learned from laboratory data is transferred to real-world electric vehicles.
Interpretability reveals both visual and numerical modalities capture physics-consistent aging patterns.
The paradigm enables accurate SOH predictions across different battery chemistries and vehicle types.

ce:section.ce:para: Electric vehicles (EVs) are being vigorously promoted worldwide as a solution to address the global energy crisis and climate challenges. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), superior in high energy density, long service life, and high working voltage, have emerged as the leading energy storage technology for EVs. 1 However, as EV mileage accumulates, LIB performance inevitably degrades due to irreversible electrochemical aging of key components such as electrodes and separators. 2 This aging process results in reduced energy storage capacity and power conversion efficiency, which directly impacts the driving range and operational performance of EVs. 3 Therefore, accurate and real-time prediction of battery state of health (SOH) is essential for enabling early detection of potential issues, facilitating proactive battery management, and ensuring safe and efficient electrified transportation. 4

Many early studies focused on estimating battery SOH through optimizing parameters of electrochemical models, 5 electric circuit models, 6 and empirical/semi-empirical models 7 via advanced filtering techniques. 8
,
9 Although traditional model-based methods have demonstrated impressive performance, their applicability is often confined to specific battery chemistries or operational conditions. 10
,
11 In contrast, data-driven methods have emerged as a mainstream alternative in recent years, owing to their flexibility in modeling complex degradation behaviors and adaptability across diverse battery systems. 12 Research efforts have been devoted into extracting degradation-sensitive features and mapping them to SOH through machine learning or deep learning techniques. Numerous features have been proposed based on the analysis of original or processed battery attributes, such as voltage, 13
,
14
,
15
,
16
,
17 current, 18
,
19
,
20 temperature, 21
,
22
,
23 electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, 24
,
25
,
26
,
27 etc. Despite the remarkable performance reported in these studies, the associated feature engineering process is often labor intensive and highly empirical, requiring extensive domain expertise and manual effort. 19

Recently, increasing attention has been directed toward applying deep learning to SOH estimation by learning latent feature representations from raw battery sensor data. 28
,
29
,
30
,
31
,
32 For instance, Ma et al. 33 proposed a hybrid convolutional neural network (CNN) and long short-term memory (LSTM) model that automatically extracts informative features from partial charging curves to predict battery capacity. Li et al. 34 incorporated an LSTM branch into a multibranch deep learning architecture for EV battery SOH prediction, directly processing raw EV sensor data without relying on handcrafted features. Studies that integrated feature engineering with deep learning have also been presented. For example, double-correlation analysis was applied to gradually decreasing current segments during the constant-voltage phase, followed by training a gated recurrent unit model for SOH estimation. 35 More recently, transformer-based models 4
,
36 have been introduced for battery SOH prediction, leveraging self-attention mechanisms to capture long-range temporal dependencies. Beyond temporal correlation modeling, advanced architectures, such as Fourier graph neural networks, have been developed to jointly model inter-series (spatial) and intra-series (temporal) dependencies within battery health-related feature sequences. 37 Meanwhile, to enhance the adaptability of deep neural network-based SOH modeling across different battery chemistries and charging/discharging conditions, transfer learning techniques 29
,
38
,
39 have been widely applied. Such learning schemes enable the transfer of knowledge from a source domain battery dataset to a distinct target domain, which is particularly meaningful when the target domain suffers from limited sample size. 40 Retraining and fine-tuning strategies 41
,
42 have also been investigated to improve the performance of pre-trained models when deployed for SOH estimation in new operating domains. Several recent studies have explored vision-inspired data-driven methods for battery SOH estimation. For instance, one strategy considered transforming one-dimensional incremental capacity curves into two-dimensional images via the Gramian angular field (GAF) algorithm, thereby enabling the use of CNNs for feature extraction and SOH estimation. 43 Zhao et al. 44 transformed partial charging current sequences into grayscale images and applied CNNs for capacity estimation. Lee et al. 45 converted capacity degradation curves into images using recurrence plots and GAF, and then applied CNNs to extract temporal features and provide more interpretable SOH estimation. In parallel, hybrid modeling methods 46
,
47 that combine physics-based models with machine learning or deep learning techniques have also gained increasing attention, aiming to leverage the interpretability of physical models and the flexibility of data-driven methods. However, the majority of data-driven studies, including hybrid methods, have been conducted based on well-designed laboratory datasets with strict data quality requirements, such as the complete data integrity, regular charging/discharging patterns, stable current rates, consistent experimental temperature, high sampling frequency, etc. 48 These idealized conditions may be challenging to replicate in real-world applications, potentially limiting the applicability of the developed methods to more complex and noisy field data. 49

Adapting existing widely discussed battery modeling methods based on laboratory data to fit real-world EV applications is an emerging yet challenging problem. 48 Based on laboratory experiments, the actual SOH values can be generally obtained using the Ampere-hour integral method 1 via fully discharging the battery after a full charge. This method presents high accuracy, which benefits from the configuration of high-precision sensors, high sampling frequency, and stable current profiles in laboratory settings. However, these ideal conditions are rarely achieved in real-world EV scenarios, where complete and stable charging/discharging processes are seldom observed. 50 For example, as depicted in Figure S1 of Note S4, the charging process of an in-use EV typically begins before the battery is fully depleted, and the voltage/current profiles exhibit strong heterogeneity. It is worth noting that, in this real-world EV dataset, the charging current is often described as a negative value, 1
,
51 which is different from the convention in most laboratory datasets where charging currents are described with positive values. To utilize EV battery data more appropriately, prior studies have mainly focused on the charging process, which is more controllable than the highly variable and unpredictable discharging process. 52 Several attempts have been made to calculate SOH for in-operation EVs using charging data and a variant of the Ampere-hour integration formula. 1 However, the resulting SOH values often exhibit substantial fluctuations that deviate from the expected monotonic degradation trend (see Figure 1 ). These inconsistencies primarily stem from multiple sources of noise and uncertainty, including errors in onboard state-of-charge (SOC) estimation, low-precision sensors, coarse data resolution, short SOC intervals, and numerical integration inaccuracies. For real-world EVs, irregular charging/discharging patterns, uncontrolled environmental conditions, and restricted data transmission capacity further complicate the development of reliable SOH prediction models. Consequently, directly applying existing data-driven methods to such field data remains challenging. 48 This fact necessitates the innovation of the existing paradigm to evolve the data-driven methods to be capable of adaptively and robustly identifying the battery SOH degradation trajectories from noisy, imperfect data collected from in-operation EVs.

To this end, this study develops a vision-powered generative paradigm for real-world EV battery SOH prediction within a cognitive modeling framework. The proposed paradigm reformulates numerical battery measurements into image-based visual contexts, enabling the identification of stable and physically meaningful SOH degradation trajectories from noisy EV measurements. This design is motivated by the proven ability of computer vision to capture local spatial correlations among image pixels, as demonstrated across diverse domains such as biomedicine 53 and autonomous driving. 54 Specifically, this paradigm leverages pixel-level spatial correlations cognitively learned from clean SOH data to guide the generation of graphical representations that effectively characterize underlying battery degradation patterns, even when the calculated SOH values exhibit significant fluctuations. The proposed paradigm consists of four steps: step 1, image synthesis based on laboratory data; step 2, deep generative model development; step 3, pixel mapping; and step 4, SOH prediction based on real EV data. In step 1, laboratory degradation data are used to generate synthetic noisy SOH curves by manually injecting controlled noise patterns to simulate real EV operating conditions. Image patches are then extracted from both the synthetic noisy curves and their corresponding clean curves. These paired noisy-clean SOH image patches are jointly used to pre-train a computer-vision-powered deep generative (CPDG) model, which learns to reconstruct clean SOH images from noisy inputs. In step 2, the CPDG model is constructed with two complementary processing channels—a visual channel and a numerical channel. The visual channel focuses on learning local spatial features from noisy SOH image patches that are critical for clean-patch generation, while the numerical channel captures temporal correlations from multiple battery health feature sequences to support the generation process. In step 3, the clean SOH image patches generated by the CPDG model are converted back into numerical SOH values through a pixel mapping procedure that quantitatively links image-level information to time series SOH predictions. In step 4, the pre-trained CPDG model is fine-tuned using a small amount of real EV battery data and subsequently deployed for SOH prediction under real-world conditions involving different battery chemistries and vehicle types. This paradigm enables an effective knowledge transfer mechanism, wherein degradation knowledge learned from extensive laboratory datasets is transferred to enhance generative modeling and prediction performance on noisy, small-sample real-world EV data.

The proposed paradigm is validated on three real-world EV battery datasets featuring different chemistries and vehicle types, as well as on a laboratory dataset in which cells are cycled under multi-step fast-charging profiles that closely approximate real EV operating conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can accurately predict the SOH trajectory for the subsequent 10 cycles based on noisy SOH observations from the preceding 100 cycles. Notably, the paradigm consistently achieves root mean-squared errors (RMSEs) below 0.0031 across all real-world EV datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art (SOTA) benchmarking methods under both controlled laboratory conditions and dynamic EV operating scenarios. By explicitly addressing data noise challenges and reducing reliance on high-quality measurements, the proposed paradigm shows strong potential for enabling a new generation of robust, data-driven battery health prognostic solutions tailored to the complex and variable operating environments of modern EVs. Moreover, the paradigm is highly adaptable to other LIB-powered systems, such as drones and industrial machinery, offering broader insights into health monitoring and reliability assessment in systems operating under complex, dynamic, and noise-prone conditions.

Four datasets, dataset I, dataset II, dataset III, and dataset IV, are utilized for model development and validation of the proposed paradigm. Dataset I is obtained from controlled laboratory experiments, whereas datasets II, III, and IV are derived from battery management systems (BMSs) installed on real-world EVs. Among them, datasets I, III, and IV are publicly accessible open-source datasets, whereas dataset II is collected through our industrial collaboration and is made publicly available in data and code availability of this study to support reproducibility and future research. A comprehensive comparison of all datasets is provided in Note S2.

Dataset I 13 comprises degradation data from 124 commercial lithium-iron-phosphate/graphite cells. In the laboratory tests, cells were cycled under various multi-step fast-charging profiles and identical constant-current discharging protocols. All cells were fully charged and fully discharged, and aging experiments were conducted in a constant-temperature chamber. Detailed cell specifications and loading profiles are provided in Note S3. In this study, 50 representative cells containing approximately 30,000 cycles and covering a diverse range of test profiles were selected for model development and validation. Additional analyses examining laboratory cell selection are provided in Note S17.

Dataset II contains approximately 4,000 equivalent cycles recorded from the battery packs of five commercial electric buses operating in Chengdu, China. According to previous studies, 55
,
56
,
57 the concept of an equivalent cycle is introduced because real-world EV batteries do not follow standardized full charging-discharging cycles as in laboratory settings. An equivalent cycle is defined as a pair of temporally adjacent charging and discharging events, regardless of whether full charge or full discharge is achieved. Each vehicle in dataset II was monitored for approximately 7 months. The battery packs are composed of over 180 serially connected lithium-ion cells. Operational data including pack voltage, single cell voltage, current, SOC, and surface temperature were collected by the onboard BMS and recorded through controller area network communication at a sampling interval of 10 s. During operation, the battery packs experienced dynamic multi-step fast-charging profiles (Note S4; Figure S1) and highly variable discharging conditions, and none of the charging-discharging events were complete cycles. Additional data processing details for dataset II are provided in Note S5.

Dataset III 58 comprises real-world operational data from 300 EVs equipped with lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide LIB packs. Each pack consists of 96 series-connected cells with a rated capacity of 155 Ah. The data were collected over a 3-year period using an onboard BMS at a sampling rate of 0.1 Hz and include key signals such as SOC, current, pack voltage, highest/lowest cell voltage, and per-cell voltages. In Liu et al., 58 battery capacity labels were derived through a combination of the Ampere-hour integration method and open-circuit voltage (OCV)-SOC calibration. 59 Detailed experimental procedures for dataset III are provided in Note S6. In this study, 16 representative vehicles were selected from dataset III for model development and validation.

Dataset IV 60 consists of real-world EV charging records collected from public charging stations. The dataset covers EVs produced by three different manufacturers, thereby providing diverse battery chemistries and operating conditions. The raw charging logs are segmented into fixed-length charging snippets using a sliding window of 128 time steps. Each snippet contains time series measurements of average cell voltage, charging current, maximum and minimum cell voltage, maximum and minimum cell temperature, SOC, and timestamps recorded during the charging process. The capacity labels in dataset IV are manually annotated by battery engineers from the corresponding manufacturers, offering high-quality ground truth information for evaluating model performance. In this study, eight representative vehicles were selected for model development and validation.

In this study, SOH is defined in a capacity-based form as the ratio between the battery current available capacity C
t and its nominal capacity C nominal. This definition is widely utilized in battery health diagnostics because capacity directly reflects the usable energy storage capability and strongly influences the achievable driving range of EVs. For dataset I, capacity values are directly measured from complete charging and discharging cycles under controlled laboratory conditions, providing high-accuracy ground truth. For real-world EV datasets II and III, the current capacity C
t is estimated from BMS operational data through the Ampere-hour integration method, 1 which is a standard and extensively validated approach in the literature. 34
,
55
,
57 Under this formulation, the SOH is computed as follows:
(Equation 1)

where the numerator represents the accumulated charge during a charging segment from time t 1 to time t 2, with the charging current I(t) recorded as negative following onboard BMS convention. The denominator represents the theoretical charge corresponding to the observed SOC change over the same segment, and the resulting SOH is a dimensionless quantity. Dataset III employs the same Ampere-hour integration principle but incorporates an additional OCV-SOC calibration 58 step to mitigate SOC drift and improve estimation reliability. For dataset IV, capacity information is manually assessed by experienced battery engineers following manufacturer-side diagnostic procedures. Although these datasets differ in the process of obtaining capacity, the resulting SOH values remain conceptually consistent because each of them quantifies the usable capacity relative to the nominal rating. This conceptual consistency ensures that the proposed paradigm can learn underlying degradation trends from noisy or partially observed capacity data without relying on any dataset-specific SOH calculation procedure.

To establish a reference standard for the authentic SOH of the battery packs studied, the empirical mode decomposition (EMD) method is applied to obtain smooth SOH residual representations for model training and performance evaluation using real EV data. The EMD has demonstrated strong capability in various signal processing applications, including the analysis of capacity degradation trajectories in battery prognostics. 1
,
61 By decomposing a time series into a set of intrinsic mode functions (IMFs) and a residual component, EMD effectively isolates the underlying long-term degradation pattern from highly dynamic and noisy observations. Figure S4 in Note S9 presents the IMFs and the residual component of noisy SOH curves. As shown in Figure S4, the residual component provides a close approximation to the smooth degradation trajectory, consistent with human interpretation and established understanding from laboratory battery aging studies. Therefore, the EMD-derived residual is adopted in this study as the reference label for the authentic SOH of real-world EV battery packs.

Figure 1 presents the SOH trajectories computed using the Ampere-hour integration formulation in Equation 1 for five EVs in dataset II, together with the corresponding EMD-extracted trends. The significant fluctuations observed in the calculated SOH values reflect the inherent characteristics of onboard BMS measurements, which are influenced by irregular charging behavior, sensor resolution limitations, and operational noise. These visualizations demonstrate both the substantial variability present in field battery SOH data and the applicability of EMD in isolating the underlying degradation pattern.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overview of the vision-powered generative paradigm
ce:section.ce:para: An overview of the proposed vision-powered generative paradigm is illustrated in Figure 2 . The following section provides a concise conceptual description of the framework, while technical details of image synthesis, numerical data processing, and model development are provided in Note S8. A detailed problem formulation of the proposed paradigm is also given in Note S1.

The CPDG model development via the proposed paradigm starts with preparing training samples via the synthesis of authentic noisy battery SOH images. To simulate the noisy SOH degradation pattern observed in real EV data, noisy SOH curve images are artificially synthesized based on the source domain laboratory data. This is achieved by manually introducing random noises into the original smooth SOH curves based on the laboratory data and then transforming them into graphical visualizations (see Figure 2B). To augment the dataset for improved model training and prediction, the sliding window method is adopted on both the original and synthesized SOH curves, splitting them into smaller image patches. A sliding window of width k cycles is applied on the synthetic SOH curve, while a larger sliding window of width (k + q) cycles is used for the original SOH curve. The patches extracted from the synthesized and real SOH curves are first calibrated to align their horizontal and vertical axes, and are then rescaled to a uniform square size. These patches are then transformed into greyscale images I
s and I
r , which form the training image pairs for the subsequent CPDG model. The CPDG aims to predict SOH values in the future q cycles based on the noisy SOH information from the past k cycles. Through a series of computational experiments (see results and discussion), the optimal q and k values are determined as 10 and 100, respectively.

The proposed CPDG model contains two channels for processing different modalities of data inputs (see Figure 2C): (1) a visual channel, which focuses on extracting local spatial features from the noisy SOH image visualizations and (2) a numerical channel, which captures degradation-relevant temporal patterns from engineered features derived from multi-source battery measurement data.

In the visual channel, the generative CNN backbone is referred to the U-Net structure, 53 which excels in high-resolution feature mapping for complex imaging tasks by preserving spatial context and integrating multi-scale information. As shown in Figure 3 , the U-Net-based CNN architecture has a symmetric encoder-decoder structure with skip connections. The synthesized noisy SOH curve image I
s serves as the channel input and is fed into the encoder. The encoder consists of four downsampling convolution modules, each comprising several convolutional layers, ReLU activation, and max-pooling layers. It extracts local spatial patterns from the noisy input image I
s and progressively compresses the image resolution to generate a lower-dimensional but high-level semantic representation R
v . To enhance its representativeness, a squeeze-and-excitation (SE) channel-attention block is integrated into the encoder, enabling the channel to adaptively reweight spatial feature channels and emphasize degradation-relevant structures in the SOH images. This feature map R
v is then concatenated with the temporal feature map R
t from the numerical data channel, forming (R
v , R
t ), which is subsequently processed by the decoder. The decoder, composed of four upsampling convolution modules, incrementally restores the image resolution by expanding the dimensionality of the fused latent representation. Before decoding, the fused map is further refined by a convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), which sequentially applies channel-attention and spatial attention operations to highlight informative degradation cues and suppress irrelevant activation patterns. The decoder finally outputs the clean SOH curve image I
r . Skip connections are employed between the encoder and decoder to facilitate the fusion of low-level and high-level spatial information, enhancing the model ability to capture multi-scale SOH degradation patterns.

In the numerical channel, a matrix of multivariate time series features derived from the partial charging process over the past k cycles is utilized as channel inputs. These features are engineered from voltage, current, temperature, SOC, charging-time profiles, and other relevant measurement signals. The detailed feature descriptions are provided in Note S7. The numerical data channel comprises a recurrent module followed by transposed-convolution upsampling modules. The recurrent module captures the underlying temporal dependencies in the historical feature sequences and encodes them into a latent feature vector representation. A bidirectional LSTM architecture is adopted, as it is well suited for modeling long-range temporal correlations while alleviating training issues such as gradient vanishing and explosion. 42 The latent feature vector is then expanded via transposed-convolution upsampling modules to form a three-dimensional temporal feature map R
t . After the upsampling stage, an SE channel-attention block is also applied to R
t to adaptively recalibrate channel-wise temporal features, enhancing the representation of degradation-relevant temporal patterns prior to the cross-branch fusion.

To further support cross-modal information integration, an inter-branch adaptive weighting attention mechanism is developed. This mechanism generates attention weights conditioned on global descriptors extracted from both the visual and numerical branches, allowing the model to dynamically adjust the relative contributions of R
v and R
t during fusion. The fused representation is subsequently forwarded to the visual decoder to generate the final SOH image output.

To convert the generated images of the CPDG model into numerical SOH representations, a pixel mapping method is proposed. For better elaboration, the relative position of an image pixel along the horizontal and vertical axes is defined as the pixel coordinate (x
p , y
p ). As shown in Figure 2C, the y-coordinate is determined according to the minimum pixel value in each column of the image. The corresponding numerical SOH value is then calculated as
(
1 −
y p
256
)
× 0.16 + a v g − 0.08
. After this pixel mapping process, 256 SOH values are obtained from the image pixel information. Next, the spline fitting method is applied on the 256 points, and k + q points are uniformly sampled at equal intervals from the fitted curve. The corresponding cycle number of these k + q points is m:m + k + q – 1. The obtained k +q points serve as the SOH reconstruction result for the past k cycles and the SOH prediction result for the future q cycles.

In the target domain EV datasets, the input images I
c for the CPDG model are derived from the calculated noisy SOH curves of each EV, while the output images I
e are obtained from the corresponding smooth EMD residual representations (Figure 2D). The proposed CPDG model is first pre-trained on the source domain laboratory dataset, after which the entire numerical channel and the visual decoder (see Figure 3) are fine-tuned and evaluated using real-world EV datasets.

This section evaluates the performance of 10-cycle-ahead SOH prediction (q = 10) using historical battery information from the preceding 100 cycles (k = 100). Prediction accuracy is assessed by averaging the results over the entire 10-cycle prediction horizon.

Figure 4 A summarizes the SOH prediction performance for eight randomly selected test cells from laboratory dataset I, evaluated using the RMSE and mean absolute error (MAE) metrics. The proposed paradigm achieves an average RMSE of 0.0013 and an average MAE of 0.0009 across all test cells, demonstrating high prediction accuracy under controlled laboratory aging conditions. To further investigate the prediction behavior at the trajectory level, Figure 4B presents comparisons between actual and predicted SOH curves for three representative test cells, together with the corresponding cycle-wise absolute error distributions shown in the inset panels. Since the initial 100 cycles of the laboratory dataset exhibit negligible capacity decay, which differs substantially from real-world EV battery degradation, these early cycles are excluded from the evaluation. Prediction therefore begins at cycle 201, with the preceding 100 cycles used as model inputs. As illustrated in Figure 4B, the developed model consistently reconstructs long-term SOH degradation trajectories over the full lifespan of each laboratory cell. Notably, for cells exhibiting pronounced knee points in their SOH evolution, the model accurately captures both the onset timing and the amplitude of these nonlinear structural changes, indicating its capability to model complex nonlinear aging dynamics rather than merely fitting smooth degradation trends. Overall, RMSE values for all test cells remain below 0.0035, underscoring the robustness and precision of the proposed paradigm for laboratory SOH prediction.

Prediction begins at cycle 101, with the preceding 100 cycles used as model inputs. Figure 4C summarizes the SOH prediction performance across three real-world EV datasets (datasets II, III, and IV), while Figure 4D presents comparisons between calculated and predic0ted SOH trajectories for one representative vehicle from each dataset. Across all three datasets, the proposed model achieves consistently low prediction errors, with RMSE values of 0.0017, 0.0027, and 0.0031 for datasets II, III, and IV, respectively. As illustrated in Figure 4D, the predicted SOH trajectories closely follow the underlying degradation patterns despite substantial variations in vehicle types and operating conditions. These results demonstrate the strong generalization capability of the proposed paradigm when deployed in heterogeneous and noise-prone real-world EV environments.

Since the proposed paradigm relies on decomposing noisy SOH measurements into an underlying degradation trend and residual noise, a systematic investigation is conducted to examine whether its predictive performance depends on the specific noise-trend separation technique employed (Note S10). In addition, the robustness of the model is further evaluated under more challenging disturbance scenarios involving diverse and non-Gaussian noise types (Note S11). The results demonstrate that the predictive performance remains stable across different noise separation strategies and noise distributions, indicating that the model captures intrinsic degradation dynamics rather than artifacts introduced by particular preprocessing methods.

Beyond nominal operating conditions, the robustness and practical reliability of the proposed paradigm are further examined under challenging scenarios, including pronounced nonlinear aging behavior associated with knee-point and sudden capacity-drop disturbances, which represent critical failure modes in safety-sensitive battery applications (Note S12). Consistent predictive performance under these conditions suggests that the model preserves reliability even when degradation trajectories undergo abrupt structural changes, indicating that it captures structurally consistent degradation dynamics rather than relying on implicit smoothness priors.

To further elucidate the design rationale of the proposed paradigm, a series of ablation studies are conducted to examine the effects of training strategy, multi-modal feature fusion, and temporal window settings (Note S13). The results demonstrate that transfer learning with targeted fine-tuning is essential for bridging the distribution gap between controlled laboratory cycling and real-world EV operation, while the integration of visual and numerical modalities provides complementary degradation information that substantially enhances prediction robustness. In addition, the influence of historical and prediction horizons on model performance reveals clear trends consistent with the slow and cumulative nature of electrochemical aging processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages of the proposed paradigm over existing methods
ce:section.ce:para: To comprehensively assess the advantages of the proposed paradigm, two tiers of comparative benchmarking experiments are conducted. These benchmarks include (1) traditional numerical fitting baselines and (2) representative data-driven benchamarks, including both classical and SOTA models.

In the first benchmarking tier, seven representative traditional numerical models are selected for comparison. These models include (1) five empirical models (a one-term exponential [OE] model, 62 a two-term exponential [TE] model, 63 a quadratic polynomial [QP] model, 64 a two-term logarithmic [TL] model, 65 and an ensemble [EN] model) 66 as well as (2) two semi-empirical models (a square-root-of-time [SRT] model 67 and a coulombic efficiency [CE] model). 7 All baseline models are fitted using the calculated SOH data, with a vertical compensation term applied to align initial 100-cycle SOH discrepancies across vehicles. Detailed mathematical formulations of these models are provided in Note S14.

Figures 5A–5C compare SOH prediction results obtained using the proposed paradigm and the traditional numerical fitting models across three real-world EV datasets. The proposed paradigm consistently aligns more closely with the reference SOH trajectory than the seven traditional numerical baselines. Empirical models, such as OE, TE, QP, and TL, tend to either over-smooth the degradation curve or diverge in regions exhibiting mild nonlinearity, while the semi-empirical SRT and CE models often underestimate the later-stage degradation rate. In contrast, the proposed method preserves both the global fading trend and local variations, yielding predictions that remain stable even under noisy field conditions. These results highlight the limitations of classical numerical fitting methods, which rely on predefined functional forms and lack flexibility when modeling complex real-world degradation behaviors. By comparison, the proposed paradigm demonstrates superior robustness and adaptability, enabling accurate SOH modeling across heterogeneous and noise-prone EV operating environments.

To further validate the superiority of the proposed method, comparative benchmarking is conducted against eight representative data-driven methods on three real-world EV datasets (datasets II, III, and IV). The selected benchmarks include classical models, support vector regression (SVR), 68 Gaussian process regression (GPR), 11 LSTM, 39 and transformer, 69 as well as recent SOTA methods, variational LSTM, 70 vision transformer (ViT), 4 sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq), 1 and SVR-RF-MLP. 71 Detailed descriptions and implementation settings for all benchmark methods are provided in Note S15.

As summarized in Table 1 , the proposed method consistently achieves the lowest RMSE and MAE across all three EV datasets, outperforming classical machine learning baselines (SVR and GPR), deep learning baselines (LSTM and transformer), as well as recent SOTA baselines (variational LSTM, ViT, Seq2Seq, and SVR-RF-MLP). Across datasets II, III, and IV, the proposed paradigm consistently attains error levels on the order of 10−3, substantially lower than those achieved by all comparison methods. The robustness of this performance across datasets characterized by diverse battery chemistries, vehicle types, and operating conditions further demonstrates the strong generalization capability of the proposed paradigm.

Figures 5D–5F presents the absolute error distributions of the proposed method and eight benchmarks across three real-world EV datasets. In all cases, the proposed method exhibits the most compact violin distributions and the lowest median errors, indicating tightly bounded and consistently smaller prediction deviations. In contrast, the benchmark methods display wider spreads and higher central tendencies, with several methods exhibiting pronounced long tails. These distributional characteristics collectively demonstrate that the proposed method achieves consistently lower and more concentrated error profiles than all competing benchmarks under diverse and noise-prone real-world operating conditions.

Beyond its predictive accuracy, the proposed paradigm also demonstrates high computational efficiency. The total end-to-end response time during deployment, including value-to-image transformation and SOH prediction, is approximately 0.58 s per sample, thereby enabling real-time SOH prediction. Detailed runtime statistics are provided in Note S16.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interpretability of visual and numerical representations
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify how the proposed framework extracts physically meaningful information from noisy data, Figure 6 visualizes the evolution of feature maps and attention weights within the visual channel for a highly noisy SOH sample. The figure tracks the processing flow from the raw SOH image (Figure 6A) through the encoder, attention, and decoder stages (Figures 6B–6H) to the final prediction, ground truth, and pixel-wise error (Figures 6I–6K), together with a spatial attention overlay (Figure 6L). This analysis reveals that the proposed modeling paradigm does not operate as a black box—it progressively suppresses stochastic disturbances while preserves the long-term degradation trajectory that reflects the underlying electrochemical aging process.

The input image in Figure 6A depicts a noisy SOH trajectory in which rapid fluctuations originate from variations in EV operating conditions, sensor noise, and estimation uncertainty. In the early encoder stages (Figures 6B–6D), these irregularities remain visible, yet a coherent downward trend begins to emerge. This suggests that the encoder progressively separates short-term disturbances from the underlying long-term capacity-fade pattern. After the CBAM module, the fused representation in Figure 6E reveals a clearer structure aligned with the global degradation trajectory. The attention mechanism concentrates weight along this path while suppressing scattered fluctuations, effectively acting as an adaptive low-pass filter. In the decoder feature maps (Figures 6F–6H), the degradation trajectory becomes progressively sharper and more continuous. Some background texture including remnants of the original noisy SOH pattern remains visible in Figures 6G and 6H due to the U-Net skip connections that reintroduce encoder information. Nonetheless, the dominant energy of the feature maps clearly follows the smooth degradation path. This indicates that the decoder selectively propagates information consistent with monotonic capacity-fade behavior.

The final prediction (Figure 6I) closely matches the smoothed ground truth SOH trajectory (Figure 6J). The pixel-wise error map in Figure 6K shows that errors remain small and are mainly confined near regions with the strongest input noise. The spatial attention overlay in Figure 6L further confirms that the model intends to assign higher attention to the smooth degradation path and lower attention to noise-dominated regions. In other words, the visual channel learns to “see through” operational and measurement noise and to reconstruct the physically meaningful capacity-fade pattern that reflects the underlying electrochemical aging.

Overall, these visualizations enable a better interpretability of the visual data processing mechanism underlying the proposed paradigm. Although the visual channel does not explicitly encode electrochemical equations governing battery aging, it provides a data-driven means of emphasizing degradation patterns that are consistent with known physical laws. Battery aging is dominated by slow, cumulative, and irreversible processes, including solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth, loss of cyclable lithium, and impedance rise, which collectively manifest as smooth and monotonic capacity-fade trajectories over long time horizons. 72 The visual feature maps in Figure 6 demonstrate that the model assigns higher weights to low frequency, smoothly evolving degradation paths while suppressing high-frequency oscillations and short-term reversals. Such fluctuations are physically implausible in electrochemical aging and mainly arise from sensor noise, SOC estimation errors, and numerical integration uncertainty in real-world EV operation. By operating in the image domain, the visual channel emphasizes the global morphology of SOH evolution rather than isolated point-wise variations, aligning naturally with the physical understanding that battery health is governed by long-term smooth and monotonic degradation trends rather than instantaneous measurements.

To further examine its adaptiveness of modeling battery degradation patterns across different battery aging stages, Figures 7A–7C presents the learned visual feature maps of a representative EV sample at the early, middle, and late aging stages. Despite stage-dependent differences in degradation level and noise characteristics, the visual channel consistently emphasizes smooth degradation trajectories while suppressing short-term fluctuations, with the degradation structure becoming more pronounced as aging progresses. This stage-resolved behavior indicates that the visual channel adaptively captures long-term degradation patterns across the battery life cycle, rather than fitting stage-specific noise or transient variations.

From a modeling perspective, such adaptive behavior can be analogous to the emphasis on time-dependent degradation trends commonly adopted in empirical battery aging models, 7
,
63
,
66 where smooth evolution is enforced through predefined functional forms or heuristic assumptions. In contrast, the proposed paradigm avoids prescribing specific empirical degradation functions, allowing degradation-relevant patterns to be learned adaptively from data while remaining consistent with established physical understanding of battery aging. In this sense, the visual channel can be interpreted as introducing a physics-consistent pattern-recognition mechanism that favors smooth and monotonic degradation behavior, while retaining the flexibility required to capture diverse degradation trajectories under real-world EV operating conditions. This design effectively bridges data-driven learning with established battery aging knowledge, without relying on explicit electrochemical modeling.

Complementary to the visual analysis, further interpretability is obtained by examining the numerical channel, whose inputs consist of physically meaningful features derived from voltage, current, and temperature measurements. Shapley additive explanations (SHAP), a model-agnostic game-theoretic method for feature attribution, 73 are employed to quantify the contribution of individual numerical features to the model predictions. Figures 7D and 7E present the SHAP-based feature interpretation results, illustrating both the overall feature importance and the stage-dependent evolution of feature contributions across different battery aging phases. Results indicate that the numerical channel of the proposed framework primarily focuses on features closely associated with well-established electrochemical stressors relevant to battery aging. Detailed descriptions of these features are provided in Table S2 in Note S7.

Among all features, F13 (the standard deviation of the temperature sequence during charging) exhibits the highest SHAP contribution, indicating that temperature variability during chargings plays a dominant role in the model predictions. This feature quantifies the extent of transient thermal fluctuations experienced by the cell. Such fluctuations have been widely reported to influence electrochemical reaction kinetics through Arrhenius-type temperature dependence and to modulate local overpotentials, 74 thereby accelerating aging-related processes such as SEI growth, electrolyte side reactions, and impedance evolution. The prominence of F13 is therefore consistent with the recognized role of dynamic thermal stress in battery aging under real-world EV operating conditions.

Features associated with charging behavior at elevated SOC levels (F6, F5, and F3) also show substantial SHAP contributions. High-SOC operation has been widely recognized as a key stress factor that intensifies parasitic degradation mechanisms, such as SEI thickening, electrolyte oxidation, and interfacial structural changes, due to increased electrode potentials and enhanced thermodynamic driving forces. 75 Moreover, the stage-resolved SHAP analysis indicates that the relative contributions of these high-SOC-related features become more pronounced during the middle-to-late aging stages, which is consistent with the physical understanding that the impact of high-SOC stress increases as aging progresses and electrode kinetics deteriorate. 76

In addition, F15 (the cell voltage measured at 80% SOC) emerges as another feature with consistently high SHAP contribution. Voltage behavior in the upper SOC region is particularly sensitive to aging-induced phenomena, including OCV curve shifts, increased polarization, and loss of cyclable lithium. 76 Therefore, voltage characteristics at high SOC have been widely used as indicators of capacity fade and impedance growth in LIBs. 1

Overall, the most influential numerical features consistently correspond to well-established electrochemical degradation drivers, including temperature-dependent reaction acceleration, parasitic processes induced by high-SOC operation, and voltage evolution associated with SEI growth and impedance increase. These observations support the physical interpretability of the numerical channel within the proposed framework, indicating that the model prioritizes physically meaningful stress-related factors rather than incidental statistical correlations.

Existing techniques for battery health prediction primarily relied on high-quality datasets collected under well-controlled laboratory conditions, which limited their applicability in real-world EV scenarios characterized by noisy measurements, irregular operating patterns, and constrained sensing accuracy. In this study, a pioneering attempt using a vision-powered generative paradigm is presented for SOH prediction of real-world EV batteries. The paradigm integrates image synthesis, CPDG model development, pixel mapping, and SOH prediction within a unified knowledge transfer framework. By coupling a visual channel that captured spatial degradation morphology with a numerical channel that modeled multivariate temporal dynamics, the generative paradigm enabled robust reconstruction and prediction of SOH trajectories from noisy field data. Extensive evaluations on three real-world EV datasets and one laboratory dataset demonstrated that the proposed method achieved highly accurate SOH prediction, with RMSE values consistently below 0.0031 across both laboratory cells and real EV fleets. Ablation studies further revealed that transfer learning and fine-tuning were critical for effective knowledge transfer from laboratory data to field conditions, while the dual-channel architecture consistently outperformed single-branch variants, confirming the complementary value of visual and numerical representations. Interpretability analyses indicated that the multi-modal model learned physics-consistent aging patterns. Additional robustness evaluations confirmed the model ability to capture complex nonlinear aging behaviors, including knee-point acceleration and sudden capacity drops.

Despite the strong performance of the proposed paradigm, several limitations remain and warrant further investigation. First, SOH labels in real-world EV deployments are typically obtained through indirect estimation rather than direct physical measurement, which may introduce label uncertainty and constrain achievable prediction accuracy. Addressing this issue is expected to further enhance model performance under the proposed paradigm. Second, although the proposed paradigm offers complementary interpretability, with the visual channel capturing macroscopic degradation trends and the numerical channel encoding physically meaningful operational signals, a direct quantitative link between the learned representations and specific electrochemical degradation mechanisms has not yet been fully established. Future work will further explore this connection to achieve more explicit model interpretability. In addition, the proposed paradigm does not explore incorporating physics-based constraints. Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) represent a promising direction for embedding physical consistency; however, applying classical PINNs to field-operated EV batteries is challenging because complete governing partial differential equations (PDEs) are difficult to formulate under highly dynamic and heterogeneous EV operating conditions. 77 Future research may therefore investigate PINN-inspired or equation-free physics-guided model architectures that introduce physical structure without requiring fully specified electrochemical equations. Finally, the real-world EV datasets considered in this study cover only a subset of practical operating scenarios. Model performance under extreme or safety-critical conditions, such as low-temperature operation, high thermal stress, and aggressive fast-charging regimes, remains to be further explored. Extending validation to these application scenarios and improving early-warning capability for abrupt degradation events constitute important directions for future research.

Overall, the proposed vision-powered generative paradigm provides a robust, interpretable, and generalizable solution for battery health prognostics under real-world EV operating conditions and offers a promising pathway for extending vision-based intelligence to a wider range of reliability and health-monitoring applications.
This study did not generate new unique materials.
